book_claims.claim: Diets high in salt increase calcium loss from bones
book_claims.mechanism: High sodium intake increases renal calcium excretion, promoting negative calcium balance and bone loss

book_claims.claim: Essential for meeting daily iodine requirement (150mcg/day) which is critical for thyroid hormone production
book_claims.mechanism: Potassium iodide in iodised salt provides iodine substrate for thyroid peroxidase enzyme to synthesize T3 and T4 hormones

book_claims.claim: Mild iodine deficiency during pregnancy and breastfeeding affects brain development of unborn children and infants, potentially reducing IQ and causing ADHD
book_claims.mechanism: Thyroid hormones are essential for neuronal migration, myelination, and synaptogenesis in developing brain

supplementary_research.finding: Lancet meta-analysis (Aburto et al., 2013; updated) confirmed reducing sodium intake to <5g salt/day significantly lowers blood pressure and reduces cardiovascular events, but iodine fortification of salt remains the most cost-effective public health intervention for iodine deficiency.

WHO global iodine status report found 29% of the world's population remains iodine-insufficient, with mild deficiency in pregnancy linked to impaired cognitive development in offspring, even in developed nations (Zimmermann & Boelaert, 2015).

Systematic review (Cappuccio & Campbell, 2017) confirmed high sodium intake increases urinary calcium excretion by approximately 40mg per 2.3g sodium, contributing to bone loss over time, particularly in postmenopausal women.

culinary_pairings.source: Natural iodine source allowing further salt reduction while maintaining iodine adequacy
Acid can partially replace salt for flavor, reducing sodium needs
Flavor complexity from herbs reduces dependence on salt for taste
Oil + salt as fundamental seasoning base
Garlic's pungency reduces need for excess salt

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: 5g iodised salt provides ~150mcg iodine (100% RDA). Bioavailability is high (~92%). Iodine content can decrease with humidity and storage; keep sealed.
WHO recommends <5g salt/day (<2g sodium). Excessive sodium increases blood pressure and renal calcium excretion (~40mg calcium lost per 2.3g sodium).
Essential for HCl production in stomach acid, nerve transmission, and fluid balance.

synergies.description: Excess salt counteracts calcium intake from vegetables by increasing calcium excretion
